description: Use-after-free in SpiderMonkey IonMonkey caused by incorrect scalar replacement of TypedArray.prototype.subarray(). CVE-2025-13024 (sec-high, MFSA 2025-87, Firefox 145). MTypedArraySubarray was statically marked alias_set=none and can_recover=true, so Ion treated every subarray() call as a pure, hoistable operation with no side effects. But subarray() on a TypedArray with inline elements MIGRATES those elements into a newly-allocated ArrayBuffer — a GC-observable allocation whose side effects invalidate the parent array's inline dense-elements pointer. When Ion hoisted subarray() over aliased element stores, the subsequent store landed on freed inline-elements storage. Under gczeal(2) (GC on every allocation), the freed storage is reused quickly enough to surface as a heap-UAF; the shell allocation-metadata builder variant asserts on metadata lookup; the oomTest variant exercises the effectful resume-after path. The fix (commit de49b52ff5f912aa3a7ea7c570ee1aafcde53fa6 by André Bargull, 2025-10-30, r=iain) converts MTypedArraySubarray to a hand-written MIR class with a scalarReplaced_ flag: getAliasSet() now returns Store(ObjectFields) unless scalar-replaced; canRecoverOnBailout() is gated on the flag; WarpCacheIRTranspiler's emitTypedArraySubarrayResult uses addEffectful + resumeAfter; and ScalarReplacement.cpp's SubarrayReplacer calls setScalarReplaced() and stealResumePoint to preserve the deoptimization point. Reporter: André Bargull.

fixes.description: Bug 1992902: Track scalar replacement for subarray. r=iain
